claims.text: WHO/Europe's statement of 30 June 2026 reports that Spain's mortality monitoring system has already estimated more than 300 heat-associated excess deaths in just a few days, that emergency medical calls in France have risen by up to 50% in some cities, that London's ambulance service recorded its highest-ever number of life-threatening emergency calls in a single day, and that Italy reported 5 deaths in 24 hours during the heatwave.

WHO/Europe's statement of 7 July 2026, titled 'Extreme heat: more deadly weeks may still lie ahead for the European Region', warns that the current heatwave risk to health remains active and that heat-health action plans save lives.

description: A record-breaking heatwave has gripped Western and Southern Europe since around 21 June 2026, straining health systems and emergency services across France, Spain, Belgium, the Netherlands, Italy and the UK. WHO/Europe issued successive public statements describing rising heat-associated mortality and warning that further dangerous heat may still lie ahead.

impact.summary: WHO/Europe's 30 June 2026 statement reported more than 300 heat-associated excess deaths in Spain within a few days, alongside a 50% surge in emergency medical calls in some French cities, the highest single-day volume of life-threatening ambulance calls ever recorded in London, and 5 deaths in 24 hours reported in Italy. WHO/Europe's follow-up statement of 7 July 2026 warned that 'more deadly weeks may still lie ahead for the European Region' as the heat continues. Media reports citing WHO leadership have put the wider European toll since 21 June at over 1,300 excess deaths, though that aggregate figure has not yet appeared in a formal WHO/Europe publication.

location.country: France (heatwave affects multiple Western European countries)
location.region: Western and Southern Europe: France, Spain, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, United Kingdom

event.scale: Continental heatwave / WHO public health emergency statement

name: 2026 European Heatwave — Public Health Emergency
